reasoning:
The baseline rate for 'Violence against civilians' in Malaysia per ACLED dataset is 0.25 events per 30-day period (3 events in the last 360 days). Assuming a Poisson process, the probability of at least one event occurring in any given 30-day window is approximately 1 - e^(-0.25) ≈ 22%. 

For the first resolution window (May 30 - June 29), 23 days have already elapsed. An extensive web search returned no reliable reports of state forces or organized armed groups committing violence against civilians in Malaysia over this period. While gang-related shootings occurred, these do not typically meet ACLED's 'Violence against civilians' criteria (there were 476 murders in <2 years in Malaysia, but ACLED only coded ~3 violence against civilian events last year). Therefore, the chance for the first date focuses mainly on an event occurring in the remaining 7 days (~6%) plus a small chance of a backlogged or obscure event over the past 23 days, yielding ~11%. 

For the second resolution date (June 22 - July 22), the window begins today, so the full base rate expectation of 22% applies. 

For the more distant dates, the probability progressively drifts slightly toward 0.5, reflecting increased uncertainty over potential longer-term shifting political or militant variables.
